meta.title: Special-population movement-safety guidance
meta.disclaimer: Educational reference based on widely accepted Pilates and rehabilitation movement-safety principles. Not medical advice. Clients with these conditions should have physician / physiotherapist clearance; the instructor's professional judgment governs all programming.
meta.classification: For each exercise: AVOID if it uses any avoid-movement or avoid-position; CAUTION if it uses a caution-movement/position; otherwise SAFE.

conditions.reference: General movement-safety principles for this condition. Educational only — not medical advice.
avoid: Loaded spinal flexion, rotation, and side bending (vertebral compression-fracture risk)
Anything with a real fall risk
utilize: Extension-focused work; keep the spine long
Neutral spine, small controlled rotation only
Props and hands-on support for stability
Low/no-impact, weight-bearing movement to load bone
conditions.programming: Favour weight-bearing and balance work to protect bone and prevent falls. Where a classical exercise would flex the spine, keep the head/chest down or work flat-back instead. Build in longer warm-ups and lighter resistance.

conditions.reference: General movement-safety principles for this condition. Educational only — not medical advice.
utilize: Extension-based work
Neutral pelvis with legs supported, or reduced range
Prop the pelvis up in sitting
Stay strictly in a pain-free range
conditions.programming: Neutral is the home base. Prioritise deep-core and gluteal stability plus thoracic extension; reintroduce flexion only in small, pain-free amounts. Avoid working to fatigue.

conditions.reference: General movement-safety principles for this condition. Educational only — not medical advice.
avoid: Rotation that may compress the facet joints
Any movement that provokes numbness, tingling, or pain
utilize: Flexion-friendly work that opens the spinal canal
Seated, quadruped, and side-lying options
Working in imprint
Support under the pelvis in any prone work
conditions.programming: These clients usually feel better in flexion and worse in extension. Emphasise gluteal strength and pain-free range; eliminate provoking rotation.

conditions.reference: General movement-safety principles for this condition. Educational only — not medical advice.
avoid: Aggressive hip-extension that arches the low back
utilize: Flexion-based work in imprint, progressing toward neutral
Pain-free positions only
Side-lying glute work; pelvic support in prone
conditions.programming: Focus on core and spinal stability in imprint→neutral, stretch hip flexors/hamstrings, strengthen glutes.

conditions.reference: General movement-safety principles for this condition. Educational only — not medical advice.
avoid: No fixed contraindications — follow the rules of any co-existing condition
Any range/resistance that exaggerates the existing curve or rotation
utilize: Neutral spine and active length (elongation)
Single-side work with controlled range
Props/wedges to support symmetry
Consistent breath work
conditions.programming: Aim to lengthen and balance left/right without reinforcing the curve; side bending and rotation will be freer in one direction than the other. Strengthen hips, glutes, and deep stabilisers.

conditions.reference: General movement-safety principles for this condition. Educational only — not medical advice.
avoid: Loaded horizontal adduction at 90° external rotation
utilize: Keep shoulder elevation low / pain-free
Neutral or externally-rotated positions
Closed-chain shoulder stability (quadruped, wall or modified plank)
Light resistance, clean mechanics
conditions.programming: Strengthen the rotator cuff and scapular stabilisers, restore good scapulo-humeral rhythm, and work in the scapular plane with the thumb up.

conditions.reference: General movement-safety principles for this condition. Educational only — not medical advice.
avoid: Lying flat on the back after the first trimester
Lying on the belly as pregnancy progresses
utilize: Deep-core and pelvic stability in neutral
Side-lying, seated, quadruped, supported standing
Slow position changes (dizziness risk)
Cooler room, more rest
conditions.programming: Requires medical clearance; stop immediately on any warning sign (pain, dizziness, bleeding, contractions, calf pain, reduced fetal movement). Focus on deep core, pelvic floor, posture, and safe balance.

conditions.reference: General movement-safety principles for this condition. Educational only — not medical advice.
avoid: Overloading the global abdominals in a way that pushes down on the pelvic floor
utilize: Coordinate a pelvic-floor lift with deep-abdominal work
Engage the floor before effort ('lift before you load')
Train the deep sling: pelvic floor + deep abdominals + multifidii
conditions.programming: Build pelvic-floor awareness and pair it with breath and deep-core work throughout; avoid global-ab overload and impact.
